walkthrough.title: Choose the Right Fascist Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Secret Hitler set up. First, find the two game boards. The Liberal board is the same for all player counts, so place it on the table. The Fascist board, however, depends on how many people are playing. There are three versions: one for five to six players, one for seven to eight, and one for nine to ten. Pick the one that matches your group and place it next to the Liberal board.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Policy Tiles
walkthrough.text: Now find the policy tiles. There are six Liberal tiles and eleven Fascist tiles. Shuffle all seventeen together face down so nobody knows which is which. Place the shuffled stack face down near the boards as the draw pile. Leave space next to it for a discard pile.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Election Tracker
walkthrough.text: Place the election tracker token on the zero space of the Fascist board. This tracker moves forward each time the table fails to elect a government. If it reaches three, a policy gets enacted automatically with no president and no chancellor.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Vote Cards
walkthrough.text: Give each player one Ja card and one Nein card. These are your voting ballots. You will use them every round to vote on whether to approve or reject the proposed government. Keep them face down in front of you.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Secret Envelopes
walkthrough.text: Now let's assign secret roles. Count your players and gather the correct number of role cards and party membership cards. For example, with seven players you need four Liberals, two Fascists, and one Hitler. Place each role card and its matching party membership card into an envelope. Shuffle the envelopes and deal one to each player. Everyone peeks at their cards secretly.

walkthrough.title: Run the Night Phase
walkthrough.text: Time for the night phase where Fascists learn who their teammates are. Everyone closes their eyes and extends a fist. The facilitator asks Fascists, not Hitler, to open their eyes and see each other. Then they identify Hitler by looking at who the facilitator indicates. In games with five or six players, Hitler also opens their eyes during this step. In games with seven or more, Hitler stays blind. Once done, everyone closes their eyes and then all players open them.

walkthrough.title: Choose the First President
walkthrough.text: Pick a random starting president. You can use any method you like, such as youngest player or drawing a card. Give that person the President placard. The presidency will pass clockwise after each election, whether it succeeds or fails.

walkthrough.title: Placards and First Turn
walkthrough.text: Place the Chancellor placard nearby for the president to hand out when they nominate someone. The starting president now nominates their first chancellor candidate, and the game begins. Remember, the previous president and previous chancellor are ineligible for the next chancellor nomination, except in five-player games where only the previous chancellor is restricted.

summary.title: Choose the First President
bullets: Random selection (youngest, card draw, etc.)
Give President placard to starting player
Presidency rotates clockwise after each election

summary.title: Placards and First Turn
bullets: Place Chancellor placard nearby
President nominates first Chancellor candidate
Term limits: prev President + prev Chancellor ineligible (5P: only prev Chancellor)

walkthrough.title: The Election Phase
walkthrough.text: Every round starts with an election. The current president nominates any eligible player as chancellor. Then the whole table discusses the nomination openly. After discussion, everyone simultaneously plays their Ja or Nein vote face down and then reveals. If the majority votes Ja, the government is formed and you move to the legislative session. If it is a tie or majority Nein, the election fails.

walkthrough.title: Failed Elections
walkthrough.text: When an election fails, the election tracker advances by one space. The presidency passes clockwise to the next player, and a new election begins. If the tracker ever reaches three, something drastic happens. The top policy tile from the draw pile is enacted automatically with no president, no chancellor, and no presidential power triggered. All term limits reset, and the tracker goes back to zero.

walkthrough.title: The Legislative Session
walkthrough.text: After a successful election, the president draws the top three policy tiles from the draw pile. The president secretly looks at them, discards one face down, and passes the remaining two to the chancellor. The chancellor then picks one of the two to enact, placing it on the matching board. The other tile is discarded face down. Neither the president nor chancellor may reveal or discuss the tiles during this process, though they may lie about what they saw afterward.

walkthrough.title: Presidential Powers
walkthrough.text: When a Fascist policy is enacted, check if the corresponding slot on the Fascist board has a presidential power. If it does, the president must use it immediately. The powers depend on your player count. They include Investigate Loyalty, where the president secretly peeks at one player's party membership card. Call Special Election, where the president picks the next president. Policy Peek, where the president sees the top three tiles of the draw pile. And Execution, where the president chooses a player to eliminate.

walkthrough.title: Investigate Loyalty
walkthrough.text: When the president investigates someone, they take that player's party membership card and look at it secretly. The president sees whether the player is a Liberal or Fascist party member. The president may then tell the table anything they want about the result. They can be honest or they can lie. The investigated player may also make counter-claims. This information is never verified publicly.

walkthrough.title: Call Special Election
walkthrough.text: The president chooses any other player to be the next president. That player immediately becomes president and runs an election. After the special election, the presidency returns to the normal clockwise rotation from the player who called it. This power can disrupt the normal flow strategically.

walkthrough.title: Execution Power
walkthrough.text: The president points at one player and that player is eliminated from the game. The executed player does not reveal their role or party membership. They simply leave the game silently. If the executed player happens to be Hitler, the Liberals win immediately. This is one of the three ways the game can end.

walkthrough.title: Veto Power
walkthrough.text: After the fifth Fascist policy is enacted, the veto power unlocks. From now on, when the chancellor receives two tiles from the president, the chancellor may say they wish to veto this agenda. If the president agrees, both tiles are discarded face down, no policy is enacted, and the election tracker advances by one. If the president refuses the veto, the chancellor must enact one of the two tiles as normal.

walkthrough.title: Chancellor Eligibility
walkthrough.text: Let's clarify who can be nominated as chancellor. The previous president and the previous chancellor from the last successful government are both ineligible. In five-player games, only the previous chancellor is restricted. Dead players can never be nominated. These term limits reset when a policy is enacted via the election tracker reaching three.

walkthrough.title: Policy Deck Management
walkthrough.text: The deck contains eleven Fascist and six Liberal tiles, so it is heavily skewed toward Fascist. This means even honest governments will sometimes be forced to enact Fascist policies. When the draw pile has fewer than three tiles remaining, shuffle the discard pile back in to form a new draw pile. The deck composition shifts as policies are enacted.

walkthrough.title: Lying and Discussion
walkthrough.text: Lying is not just allowed in Secret Hitler, it is essential. Players may freely lie about their role, their party, what tiles they saw, and what they discarded. You may accuse anyone of anything at any time. The only thing you cannot do is show your role or party cards to prove your identity. All accusations and defenses happen through persuasion alone.

walkthrough.title: How the Game Ends
walkthrough.text: There are three ways for the game to end. Liberals win if five Liberal policies are enacted or if Hitler is executed. Fascists win if six Fascist policies are enacted or if Hitler is elected chancellor after three or more Fascist policies are already on the board. When the game ends, all players reveal their roles and discuss the deceptions that took place.

bullets: President nominates Chancellor → table discusses → simultaneous Ja/Nein vote
Majority Ja → government formed → legislative session
Majority Nein or tie → election fails

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your First Game
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Secret Hitler! We will walk through a scripted practice round with seven players. We have four Liberals, two Fascists, and one Hitler. The Fascists know each other and know who Hitler is, but Hitler does not know who the Fascists are. Let's see how a few rounds play out.

walkthrough.title: Night Phase Complete
walkthrough.text: Everyone has peeked at their secret role. The night phase is done. The two Fascists opened their eyes and spotted each other and Hitler. Hitler kept their eyes closed the whole time. Now everyone opens their eyes and we are ready to begin. Player 1 has been randomly chosen as the first president.

walkthrough.title: First Nomination
walkthrough.text: Player 1, the president, looks around the table and nominates Player 3 as chancellor. The table discusses briefly. Some players shrug, others nod. Since nobody has any information yet, this feels like a coin flip. Everyone plays their vote cards face down.

walkthrough.title: Vote Result
walkthrough.text: Let's flip the votes. Five Ja and two Nein. The government passes. Player 1 draws three policy tiles from the draw pile. They look at them secretly. Let's say Player 1 drew two Fascist tiles and one Liberal tile. Player 1 discards one Fascist tile and passes the remaining two, one Liberal and one Fascist, to Chancellor Player 3.

walkthrough.title: Chancellor Enacts a Policy
walkthrough.text: Player 3, the chancellor, looks at the two tiles. They see one Liberal and one Fascist. Player 3 enacts the Liberal policy and places it on the Liberal board. The table cheers. One down, four to go for the Liberals. The election tracker resets to zero, and the presidency passes clockwise to Player 2.

walkthrough.title: Second Election
walkthrough.text: Player 2 is now president. They nominate Player 5 as chancellor. The table discusses again. The vote goes through with six Ja votes. Player 2 draws three tiles and gets three Fascist tiles. That is rough luck but not surprising given the deck has eleven Fascist and only six Liberal. Player 2 has no choice but to pass two Fascist tiles to Player 5.

walkthrough.title: A Forced Fascist Policy
walkthrough.text: Player 5 receives two Fascist tiles. There is nothing they can do. They enact one Fascist policy and place it on the Fascist board. Player 2 announces to the table that they drew three Fascist tiles and had no Liberal option. But wait, can we trust that? This is where the game gets interesting.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Mistake: Blind Trust
walkthrough.text: Player 4, a Liberal, immediately announces that they believe Player 2 completely. Player 4 says anyone who drew three Fascist tiles obviously had no choice and that Player 2 is clearly Liberal. This is a mistake. There is no way to verify what the president drew. Even if Player 2 is telling the truth this time, blindly trusting legislative claims sets a dangerous precedent.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Track, Don't Trust
walkthrough.text: The lesson here is to track claims rather than blindly trust them. When someone says they drew three Fascist, note it down mentally. Compare it against the overall tile count as the game progresses. If too many presidents claim all-Fascist draws and the math does not add up, someone is lying. Trust is earned over multiple rounds of consistent behavior, not given away after one claim.

walkthrough.title: Third Election Heats Up
walkthrough.text: Player 3 is now president. They nominate Player 6 as chancellor. Some players are suspicious of Player 6 because they voted Nein earlier. The vote is close but passes with four Ja and three Nein. Player 3 draws two Fascist and one Liberal. They pass the Liberal and one Fascist to Player 6. Player 6 enacts the Liberal policy. Two Liberal policies down.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Mistake: Always Voting Ja
walkthrough.text: Looking back at the first three elections, Player 4 has voted Ja on every single one without hesitation. Player 4 figures that approving governments moves the game forward. But this is a mistake. Approving every government means Fascists get easy access to the legislative session. As more Fascist policies pile up, blindly approving governments becomes increasingly dangerous.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Use Your Nein Vote
walkthrough.text: Your Nein vote is a weapon. Use it to block suspicious pairings, especially once three or more Fascist policies are on the board. Voting Nein on a pairing that includes a suspected Fascist or a potential Hitler is critical for Liberal survival. The election tracker advancing is a cost, but it is often worth paying to block a dangerous government. Be strategic with your votes.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Mistake: Wasting Execution
walkthrough.text: Let's jump ahead. A fourth Fascist policy has been enacted, triggering an execution power. Player 4 happens to be president. Without much analysis, Player 4 picks a random player to execute, saying they just have a gut feeling. Unfortunately, they execute a fellow Liberal. Random executions are a coin flip at best, and they just weakened their own team.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Execution Needs Evidence
walkthrough.text: Execution is the most powerful tool in the game. Do not waste it on a gut feeling. Before you execute someone, review the evidence. Who had conflicting legislative claims? Who has been voting suspiciously? Who has been positioned for chancellor by known or suspected Fascists? Use investigation results, voting patterns, and legislative conflicts to make an informed decision. A data-driven execution can win the game. A random one usually hurts the Liberals.

walkthrough.title: Redemption: Smart Play
walkthrough.text: Let's rewind that execution. This time, Player 4 reviews the game so far. Player 2 claimed three Fascist draws, and Player 5 confirmed receiving two Fascist tiles. But Player 7 was in a government that also enacted Fascist, and their chancellor contradicted their tile claims. Player 4 executes Player 7 based on the legislative conflict. This is a much smarter play, whether or not it hits a Fascist.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations, you have just walked through your first practice rounds of Secret Hitler! You have seen how elections work, how the legislative session creates information and deception, and how Player 4 learned three important lessons. Track claims instead of trusting blindly, use your Nein vote strategically, and base executions on evidence rather than gut feelings.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Play
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy advice on playing as Liberal, Fascist, or Hitler.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to terms, powers, and team compositions.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Liberal Strategy Basics
walkthrough.text: As a Liberal, your job is to figure out who the Fascists are while passing Liberal policies. Pay close attention to voting patterns. Fascists will often vote Ja to governments that include their teammates and Nein to governments that would help Liberals. Track who votes with whom over multiple rounds. Also track legislative claims carefully. When a president and chancellor disagree about what tiles were drawn, one of them is lying.

walkthrough.title: Fascist Strategy Basics
walkthrough.text: As a Fascist, you need to pass Fascist policies while appearing Liberal. The best Fascists blend in perfectly during the early game. Vote like a Liberal would. Build trust by occasionally passing Liberal policies when you are in government. Your real goal is to create confusion and division among the Liberals. When two Liberals start accusing each other based on a legislative conflict you manufactured, you are winning.

walkthrough.title: Hitler Strategy Basics
walkthrough.text: Hitler has a unique role. You should play as the most trustworthy person at the table. Avoid suspicion at all costs, because being investigated or executed ends the game for your team. In seven-plus player games, you do not even know who your Fascist teammates are, so genuinely play Liberal in the early rounds. Your Fascists will try to get you elected chancellor after three Fascist policies are on the board. Let them set it up.

walkthrough.title: Reading Voting Patterns
walkthrough.text: Voting patterns reveal more than legislative claims. Track who votes Ja and Nein on every election. Fascists tend to support governments that include their teammates. If two players consistently vote the same way, especially in controversial elections, they might be on the same team. A player who always votes Ja is not necessarily Liberal. They might be a Fascist trying to get every government through to maximize their chances at the legislative session.

walkthrough.title: The Tile Count Strategy
walkthrough.text: Keep a running count of how many Liberal and Fascist tiles have been enacted and discarded. The deck starts with six Liberal and eleven Fascist tiles. If four Fascist tiles have been enacted but six presidents have claimed to draw all Fascist, the math does not add up. Someone lied about discarding a Liberal tile. This arithmetic approach cuts through emotional arguments and gives you hard data to work with.

walkthrough.title: Handling the Election Tracker
walkthrough.text: The election tracker is a pressure valve. Three consecutive failed elections auto-enact the top policy, which could be Liberal or Fascist with no one controlling it. Sometimes it is worth letting a mediocre government through to avoid the tracker advancing. Other times, blocking a dangerous government is worth the tracker cost. As a general rule, block aggressively once three or more Fascist policies are down, because the stakes of electing Hitler as chancellor are too high.

walkthrough.title: Common Bluffing Techniques
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about common lies and how to spot them. A Fascist president may claim they drew three Fascist tiles when they actually had a Liberal. A Fascist chancellor may enact Fascist and blame the president for passing two Fascist tiles. An honest Liberal government that drew poorly looks identical to a lying Fascist one. The way to differentiate is consistency. Track multiple data points across rounds. One suspicious round is ambiguous. Two suspicious rounds with the same player is a pattern.

walkthrough.title: Late Game Survival
walkthrough.text: Once three or more Fascist policies are on the board, the game shifts dramatically. Now electing Hitler as chancellor wins it for the Fascists. Every chancellor nomination becomes critical. Block any player you are not reasonably confident is Liberal. If you have execution power, use it wisely based on accumulated evidence. And if veto power is active, remember that every vetoed agenda advances the election tracker. The late game is a knife fight, so play tight and trust your data.

summary.title: Liberal Strategy Basics
bullets: Track voting patterns — Fascists support teammate governments
Track legislative claims — disagreements between President/Chancellor reveal lies
Build trust slowly through consistent Liberal policy enactment

summary.title: Fascist Strategy Basics
bullets: Blend in — vote and behave like a Liberal early on
Create confusion between Liberals via manufactured conflicts
Occasionally pass Liberal policies to build trust

summary.title: Hitler Strategy Basics
bullets: Play as the most trustworthy person at the table
Avoid suspicion — investigation or execution loses for Fascists
In 7+P games, you're blind — genuinely play Liberal early

summary.title: Reading Voting Patterns
bullets: Track every Ja/Nein on every election
Consistent voting alignment suggests same team
Always-Ja player could be Fascist maximizing legislative access

summary.title: The Tile Count Strategy
bullets: Track enacted + discarded tiles vs. deck composition (6 Lib, 11 Fas)
If claims don't match the math, someone lied
Arithmetic cuts through emotional arguments

summary.title: Handling the Election Tracker
bullets: 3 failed elections → auto-enact top tile (uncontrolled)
Sometimes let mediocre govts through to avoid tracker
Block aggressively after 3+ Fascist policies (Hitler risk)

summary.title: Common Bluffing Techniques
bullets: Fascist President: claim all-Fascist draw to hide discarded Liberal
Fascist Chancellor: enact Fascist, blame President for bad pass
Differentiate via consistency — 1 bad round = ambiguous, 2 = pattern

summary.title: Late Game Survival
bullets: After 3+ Fascist policies, every Chancellor nomination is critical
Block anyone not reasonably confirmed Liberal
Use execution based on data, not gut; veto advances tracker

walkthrough.title: The Information Economy
walkthrough.text: Secret Hitler is fundamentally an information game. Every action generates data: who nominated whom, who voted how, what tiles were claimed, and which policies were enacted. The best players treat this data as currency. Track every data point, because patterns emerge over multiple rounds. A single suspicious action is noise. Two or three suspicious actions from the same player are a signal.

walkthrough.title: Conflict Creation as Fascist
walkthrough.text: The most powerful Fascist strategy is engineering a conflict between two Liberals. When you are president, discard the Liberal tile and pass two Fascist tiles to a Liberal chancellor. The chancellor is forced to enact Fascist. Now claim you drew two Fascist and one Liberal, and that you passed the Liberal tile. The Liberal chancellor will contradict you, saying they received two Fascist tiles. The table cannot determine who is lying, and two Liberals are now at each other's throats.

walkthrough.title: Soft Power and Positioning
walkthrough.text: As a Fascist, positioning Hitler for a chancellor nomination after three Fascist policies is the primary win condition. Do this subtly by building trust for Hitler across multiple rounds. Nominate Hitler for early governments when the stakes are low. If Hitler passes a Liberal policy in an early government, the table will view them favorably later. Then when the critical moment comes, the table is more likely to approve Hitler as chancellor.

walkthrough.title: Defensive Liberal Play
walkthrough.text: Strong Liberal play after three Fascist policies means being very selective about who becomes chancellor. The simplest rule: only approve chancellors who have enacted Liberal policies in previous governments. If you have investigation results, weight them heavily. If there is a confirmed Liberal through investigation or consistent legislative behavior, pair them into government. Do not take risks on unknown players when Hitler's election wins the game.

walkthrough.title: Advanced Voting Strategy
walkthrough.text: Your vote conveys information even beyond pass or fail. Voting Nein on a government that passes tells the table you are suspicious of that pairing. Voting Ja on a government that fails tells the table you trusted it. Advanced players use their votes to send signals. A Liberal might vote Nein on a government they actually think is fine, just to see how the other players react. Watch who changes their behavior based on your vote.

walkthrough.title: Special Election Tactics
walkthrough.text: Call Special Election is one of the most powerful plays in the game. As a Liberal president, use it to put a confirmed Liberal into the presidency. As a Fascist, use it to skip over a Liberal president in the normal rotation or to position a Fascist teammate for a key legislative session. The disruption to the normal rotation can throw off the table's plans entirely. Remember that after the special election, the rotation returns to the player after the one who called it.

walkthrough.title: Execution Timing
walkthrough.text: The execution power is most valuable when you have strong evidence. If two players had a legislative conflict, one of them is lying. Executing the player who seems more suspicious based on other data points is the optimal play. But execution also has a secondary value: it removes a player from the game entirely, reducing the number of unknown roles. In high-player-count games, strategically reducing the player count can help Liberals gain certainty faster.

walkthrough.title: Veto Strategy
walkthrough.text: Veto power creates a new layer of strategy. As a Liberal chancellor, vetoing a double-Fascist pass is defensible. As a Fascist chancellor, vetoing a pass that includes a Liberal can stall Liberal progress while advancing the election tracker toward a chaotic auto-enact. As president, agreeing to a veto shows trust in the chancellor. Refusing a veto forces a policy through but makes you look suspicious if the chancellor was being honest about the tiles. Every veto decision carries information.

walkthrough.title: Meta-Game Awareness
walkthrough.text: Experienced players develop table reads that go beyond the game mechanics. Watch body language, hesitation speed, and speech patterns. Players who suddenly become quiet after a legislative session may be hiding something. Players who are overly emphatic about their innocence may be compensating. The meta-game is not about any single tell. It is about noticing changes in a player's normal behavior. When someone acts differently from their baseline, ask yourself why.

walkthrough.title: Player Count Adjustments
walkthrough.text: Adjust your strategy based on player count. In five to six player games, Hitler knows who the Fascist is, making Fascist coordination easier but the smaller group makes deception harder. In seven to eight player games, Hitler is blind and there are two Fascists plus Hitler, creating complex dynamics. In nine to ten player games, three Fascists plus a blind Hitler means the Fascist team has numbers but coordination challenges. Liberals should play more aggressively in higher player counts because there are more threats to identify.

summary.title: The Information Economy
bullets: Track every nomination, vote, claim, and enactment
Single suspicious action = noise; multiple = signal
Treat data as currency — best-informed player wins

summary.title: Conflict Creation as Fascist
bullets: Discard Liberal, pass 2 Fascist to Liberal Chancellor
Claim you passed the Liberal → Chancellor contradicts
Two Liberals now fight each other — Fascist wins

bullets: Build trust for Hitler in early low-stakes governments
Hitler passes Liberal early → table trusts Hitler later
Position Hitler for Chancellor after 3+ Fascist policies

summary.title: Defensive Liberal Play
bullets: After 3 Fascist policies: only approve confirmed-Liberal Chancellors
Pair confirmed Liberals into government
Don't risk unknown players when Hitler election = instant loss

summary.title: Advanced Voting Strategy
bullets: Votes convey information beyond pass/fail
Strategic Nein signals suspicion to other players
Watch how others react to your votes

summary.title: Special Election Tactics
bullets: Liberal: put confirmed Liberal into presidency
Fascist: skip Liberal in rotation, position teammate
Disrupts plans — rotation resumes after caller

summary.title: Execution Timing
bullets: Execute based on legislative conflicts + supporting evidence
Secondary value: reduces unknowns in high-player games
Best when paired with investigation results

summary.title: Veto Strategy
bullets: Liberal Chancellor: veto double-Fascist defensibly
Fascist Chancellor: veto to stall Liberal progress + advance tracker
Every veto decision carries information for the table

summary.title: Meta-Game Awareness
bullets: Watch body language, hesitation, speech pattern changes
No single tell — look for deviations from player baseline
Quiet after legislation or over-emphatic defense = potential signals

summary.title: Player Count Adjustments
bullets: 5-6P: Hitler knows Fascist, smaller group → harder to hide
7-8P: Hitler blind, 2 Fascists → complex dynamics
9-10P: 3 Fascists but coordination harder; Liberals play more aggressively

entries.definition: The majority team. Liberals win by enacting five Liberal policies or by executing Hitler. Liberals do not know anyone's role.
The minority team. Fascists know each other and know who Hitler is. They win by enacting six Fascist policies or getting Hitler elected Chancellor after three or more Fascist policies.

A special Fascist role. In 5-6 player games, Hitler knows who the Fascist is. In 7+ player games, Hitler is blind and does not know their teammates. If Hitler is elected Chancellor after 3+ Fascist policies, Fascists win. If Hitler is executed, Liberals win.
The player who nominates a Chancellor and draws three policy tiles during the legislative session. The presidency rotates clockwise after each election.
The player nominated by the President who receives two policy tiles and enacts one. The previous Chancellor is term-limited from the next nomination.
Yes and No vote cards. All players vote simultaneously to approve or reject the proposed government. Majority Ja passes; majority Nein or tie fails.
A counter that advances by one each time an election fails. At three, the top policy is auto-enacted with no government, no power, and all term limits reset.
The previous President and previous Chancellor are ineligible for Chancellor nomination. In 5-player games, only the previous Chancellor is limited. Limits reset on tracker-3 auto-enact.
After a successful election, the President draws 3 tiles, discards 1, and passes 2 to the Chancellor. The Chancellor enacts 1 and discards 1. No communication allowed during the session.
17 total tiles: 11 Fascist and 6 Liberal. Shuffled into a face-down draw pile. When fewer than 3 remain, the discard pile is reshuffled in.
Presidential power. The President secretly views one player's party membership card (Liberal or Fascist). The President may lie about the result.
Presidential power. The President picks any player as the next President. After that election, the rotation resumes from the player after the one who called it.
Presidential power. The President secretly looks at the top 3 tiles of the draw pile. They may lie about what they saw.
Presidential power. The President eliminates one player from the game. The executed player does not reveal their role. If the target is Hitler, Liberals win immediately.
Unlocked after the 5th Fascist policy. The Chancellor may propose vetoing the agenda. If the President agrees, both tiles are discarded and the election tracker advances by one.
A card showing whether a player belongs to the Liberal or Fascist party. Used during Investigate Loyalty. Cannot be shown voluntarily to prove identity.
The opening phase where Fascists identify each other and Hitler. All players close eyes, Fascists (not Hitler) open eyes to see teammates and Hitler. In 5-6P games, Hitler also opens eyes.
Tracks enacted Liberal policies. Five slots. When all five are filled, Liberals win immediately. Same board for all player counts.
Tracks enacted Fascist policies. Six slots with presidential powers that vary by player count (5-6, 7-8, 9-10). When all six are filled, Fascists win.
When the President and Chancellor give contradictory accounts of what tiles were drawn and passed. One or both are lying. A key source of information for the table.
